content: If $A=\left[\begin{array}{rrr}1 & -2 & 2 \\ 0 & 2 & -3 \\ 3 & -2 & 4\end{array}\right]$ then $A(I+\operatorname{adj} A)=$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & A(I+\operatorname{adj} A) \\ & =A I+A(\operatorname{adj} A) \\ & =A+|A| I \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\quad \ldots[\because A(\operatorname{adj} A)=|A| I]\end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} |A| & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -2 & 2 \\ 0 & 2 & -3 \\ 3 & -2 & 4 \end{array}\right| \\ & =1(2)+2(9)+2(-6) \\ & =8 \\ A(I+\operatorname{adj} A) & =\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -2 & 2 \\ 0 & 2 & -3 \\ 3 & -2 & 4 \end{array}\right]+8\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] \\ & =\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -2 & 2 \\ 0 & 2 & -3 \\ 3 & -2 & 4 \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 8 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 8 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 8 \end{array}\right] \\ & =\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 9 & -2 & 2 \\ 0 & 10 & -3 \\ 3 & -2 & 12 \end{array}\right] \end{aligned} $$

content: The vectors $\bar{p}=\hat{i}+a \hat{j}+a^2 \hat{k}, \bar{q}=\hat{i}+b \hat{j}+b^2 \hat{k}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{r}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{c} \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\mathrm{c}^2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$ are non-coplanar and $\left|\begin{array}{lll}a & a^2 & 1+a^3 \\ b & b^2 & 1+b^3 \\ c & c^2 & 1+c^3\end{array}\right|=0$ then the value of $(a b c)$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Since } \overline{\mathrm{p}}, \overline{\mathrm{q}}, \overline{\mathrm{r}} \text { are non-coplanar, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & a & a^2 \\ 1 & b & b^2 \\ 1 & c & c^2 \end{array}\right| \neq 0 \quad \ldots(i) \\ & \left|\begin{array}{lll} a & a^2 & a^3+1 \\ b & b^2 & b^3+1 \\ c & c^2 & c^3+1 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{lll} a & a^2 & a^3 \\ b & b^2 & b^3 \\ c & c^2 & c^3 \end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{lll} a & a^2 & 1 \\ b & b^2 & 1 \\ c & c^2 & 1 \end{array}\right|=0 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

By taking $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ common from $\mathrm{R}_1, \mathrm{R}_2, \mathrm{R}_3$ respectively in the $1^{\text {st }}$ det., we get $a b c\left|\begin{array}{lll}1 & a & a^2 \\ 1 & b & b^2 \\ 1 & c & c^2\end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{lll}a & a^2 & 1 \\ b & b^2 & 1 \\ c & c^2 & 1\end{array}\right|=0$

Applying $\mathrm{C}_2 \leftrightarrow \mathrm{C}_3$ in $2^{\text {nd }}$ det., we get $a b c\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & a & a^2 \\ 1 & b & b^2 \\ 1 & c & c^2\end{array}\right|-\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a & 1 & a^2 \\ b & 1 & b^2 \\ c & 1 & c^2\end{array}\right|=0$

Applying $\mathrm{C}_1 \leftrightarrow \mathrm{C}_2$ in $2^{\text {nd }}$ det., we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & a b c\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & a & a^2 \\ 1 & b & b^2 \\ 1 & c & c^2 \end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & a & a^2 \\ 1 & b & b^2 \\ 1 & c & c^2 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(1+a b c)\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & a & a^2 \\ 1 & b & b^2 \\ 1 & c & c^2 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 1+a b c=0 \,\,\,\,\,\,…[From (i)]\\ & \Rightarrow a b c=-1 \end{aligned} $$

content: If $A$ is a matrix of order 2 and $I$ is the identity matrix of order 2 such that $A^2-4 A+3 I=0$ then $(A+3 I)^{-1}=$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & A^2-4 A+3 I=0 \\ & \Rightarrow A^2+3 A-7 A+3 I=0 \\ & \Rightarrow A^2+3 A-7 A-21 I+24 I=0 \\ & \Rightarrow A(A+3 I)-7 I(A+3 I)=-24 I \\ & \Rightarrow(A+3 I)(A-7 I)=-24 I \\ & \Rightarrow(A+3 I)\left(\frac{7 I}{24}-\frac{A}{24}\right)=I \end{aligned} $$

$$ \Rightarrow(A+3 I)^{-1}=\frac{7 I}{24}-\frac{A}{24} $$

content: Matrix A is non-singular matrix and $(A-3 I)(A-5 I)=0$, then $\frac{15}{8} A^{-1}=\ldots \ldots$
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & (A-3 I)(A-5 I)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow A^2-8 A+15 I=0 \\ & \Rightarrow A^2 A^{-1}-8 A A^{-1}+15 I A^{-1}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow A-8 I+15 A^{-1}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 15 A^{-1}=8 I-A \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{15}{8} A^{-1}=I-\frac{1}{8} A \end{aligned} $$

content: If $\mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}5 \mathrm{a} & -\mathrm{b} \\ 3 & 2\end{array}\right]$ and A .adj $\mathrm{A}=\mathrm{AA}^{\mathrm{T}}$, then $5 \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}=$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & A_{11}=(-1)^{1+1}(2)=2, A_{12}=(-1)^{1+2}(3)=-3 \\ & A_{21}=(-1)^{2+1}(-b)=b, A_{22}=(-1)^{2+2}(5 a)=5 a \\ \therefore &\,\,\, \operatorname{adj} A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & -3 \\ b & 5 a \end{array}\right]^T=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & b \\ -3 & 5 a \end{array}\right] \end{aligned} $$

Given, $\mathrm{A} \operatorname{adj} \mathrm{A}=\mathrm{AA}^{\mathrm{T}}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 \mathrm{a} & -\mathrm{b} \\ 3 & 2 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & \mathrm{~b} \\ -3 & 5 \mathrm{a} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 \mathrm{a} & -\mathrm{b} \\ 3 & 2 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 \mathrm{a} & 3 \\ -\mathrm{b} & 2 \end{array}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc} 10 \mathrm{a}+3 \mathrm{~b} & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \mathrm{~b}+10 \mathrm{a} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 25 \mathrm{a}^2+\mathrm{b}^2 & 15 \mathrm{a}-2 \mathrm{~b} \\ 15 \mathrm{a}-2 \mathrm{~b} & 13 \end{array}\right] \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore$ by the equality of matrices,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \quad 15 a-2 b=0 \text { and } 3 b+10 a=13 \\ & \quad \Rightarrow a=\frac{2}{5} \text { and } b=3 \\ & \therefore \quad 5 a+b=5\left(\frac{2}{5}\right)+3=2+3=5 \end{aligned} $$

content: If $A=\left[\begin{array}{lll}3 & -3 & 4 \\ 2 & -3 & 4 \\ 0 & -1 & 1\end{array}\right]_{3 \times 3}$, then $A^{-1}=$
explanation:
$$ |A|=\left|\begin{array}{lll} 3 & -3 & 4 \\ 2 & -3 & 4 \\ 0 & -1 & 1 \end{array}\right|=1 \neq 0 $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \operatorname{adj} A & =\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -2 & -2 \\ -1 & 3 & 3 \\ 0 & -4 & -3 \end{array}\right]^{\top} \\ & =\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & 0 \\ -2 & 3 & -4 \\ -2 & 3 & -3 \end{array}\right] \\ \therefore \quad A^{-1} & =\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & 0 \\ -2 & 3 & -4 \\ -2 & 3 & -3 \end{array}\right] \\ A^2 & =\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 3 & -3 & 4 \\ 2 & -3 & 4 \\ 0 & -1 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 3 & -3 & 4 \\ 2 & -3 & 4 \\ 0 & -1 & 1 \end{array}\right] \\ & =\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 3 & -4 & 4 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ -2 & 2 & -3 \end{array}\right] \\ A^3 & =A^2 \cdot A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 3 & -4 & 4 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ -2 & 2 & -3 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 3 & -3 & 4 \\ 2 & -3 & 4 \\ 0 & -1 & 1 \end{array}\right] \\ & =\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & 0 \\ -2 & 3 & -4 \\ -2 & 3 & -3 \end{array}\right] \\ & =A^{-1} \end{aligned} $$

content: If $A=\left[\begin{array}{rrr}1 & -1 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 & -3 \\ 2 & 1 & 0\end{array}\right], B=\operatorname{adj} A$ and $C=5 A$, then $\frac{|\operatorname{adjB}|}{|\mathrm{C}|}=$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 & -3 \\ 2 & 1 & 0 \end{array}\right] \\ & B=\operatorname{adj} A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 3 & 1 & 1 \\ -6 & -2 & 3 \\ -4 & -3 & 2 \end{array}\right] \\ & \therefore\, \operatorname{adj} B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -5 & 5 \\ 0 & 10 & -15 \\ 10 & 5 & 0 \end{array}\right]=5 A \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad \operatorname{adj} \mathrm{B}=\mathrm{C}$$\,\,\,\,\,\ldots .[\because \mathrm{C}=5 \mathrm{~A}$ (given) $]$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow|\operatorname{adj} B|=|C| \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{|\operatorname{adj} B|}{|C|}=1 \end{aligned} $$

content: If A and B are non-singular matrices of order 2 such that $\quad(A B)^{-1}=\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{cc}-1 & -3 \\ 2 & 3\end{array}\right] \quad$ and $A^{-1}=\frac{1}{3}\left[\begin{array}{cc}4 & 3 \\ -1 & 0\end{array}\right]$ then $B^{-1}=$
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & (\mathrm{AB})^{-1}=\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{cc} -7 & -3 \\ 2 & 3 \end{array}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{B}^{-1} \mathrm{~A}^{-1}=\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{cc} -7 & -3 \\ 2 & 3 \end{array}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{B}^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{3}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 4 & 3 \\ -1 & 0 \end{array}\right]\right)=\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{cc} -7 & -3 \\ 2 & 3 \end{array}\right] \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Only option (C) satisfies the above condition. } \end{aligned} $$

content: If matrix $\quad A=\frac{1}{11}\left[\begin{array}{rrr}-1 & 7 & -24 \\ 2 & a & 4 \\ 2 & -3 & 15\end{array}\right] \quad$ and $A^{-1}=\left[\begin{array}{rrr}3 & 3 & 4 \\ 2 & -3 & 4 \\ b & -1 & c\end{array}\right]$, then the values of $a, b, c$ respectively are ……
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{AA}^{-1}=\mathrm{I} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{11}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -1 & 7 & -24 \\ 2 & \mathrm{a} & 4 \\ 2 & -3 & 15 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 3 & 3 & 4 \\ 2 & -3 & 4 \\ \mathrm{~b} & -1 & \mathrm{c} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{11}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 11-24 \mathrm{~b} & 0 & 24-24 \mathrm{c} \\ 6+2 \mathrm{a}+4 \mathrm{~b} & 2-3 \mathrm{a} & 8+4 \mathrm{a}+4 \mathrm{c} \\ 15 \mathrm{~b} & 0 & -4+15 \mathrm{c} \end{array}\right] =\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] \end{aligned}\\ &\text { By equality of matrices, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & 15 b=0 \Rightarrow b=0 \\ & 2-3 a=11 \Rightarrow a=-3 \\ & 24-24 c=0 \Rightarrow c=1 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: If $A=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & \cot \frac{\theta}{2} \\ -\cot \frac{\theta}{2} & 1\end{array}\right]$ then $A^{-1}=$
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & |A|=1+\cot ^2 \frac{\theta}{2}=\operatorname{cosec}^2 \frac{\theta}{2} \neq 0 \\ & \text { Now, } \operatorname{adj}(A)=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -\cot \frac{\theta}{2} \\ \cot \frac{\theta}{2} & 1 \end{array}\right] \\ \therefore \quad & A^{-1}=\frac{1}{|A|}(\operatorname{adj}(A))=\frac{1}{\operatorname{cosec}^2 \frac{\theta}{2}}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -\cot \frac{\theta}{2} \\ \cot \frac{\theta}{2} & 1 \end{array}\right] \\ & =\sin ^2 \frac{\theta}{2} A^T=\left(\frac{1-\cos \theta}{2}\right) A^T \end{aligned} $$

content: Let A be a non-singular matrix of order n and $|A|=k$, then $(\operatorname{adj} A)^{-1}$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & |A|=k \neq 0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, …[Given\\ \therefore \quad & A^{-1}=\frac{1}{|A|}(\operatorname{adj} A) \\ & A^{-1}=\frac{(\operatorname{adj} A)}{k} \end{aligned} $$

]

k. $\mathrm{A}^{-1}=(\operatorname{adj} \mathrm{A})$

Taking inverse on both sides, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \left(k A^{-1}\right)^{-1}=(\operatorname{adj} A)^{-1} \\ & \Rightarrow k^{-1}\left(A^{-1}\right)^{-1}=(\operatorname{adj} A)^{-1} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}^{-1} \mathrm{~A}=(\operatorname{adj} \mathrm{A})^{-1} \quad \ldots\left(\because\left(\mathrm{~A}^{-1}\right)^{-1}=\mathrm{A}\right) $$

$$ \Rightarrow \frac{A}{k}=(\operatorname{adj} A)^{-1} $$

content: The third element in the second row of adjoint of a matrix $A=\left[a_{i j}\right]_{3 \times 3}$ (where $a_{i j}=2 i+j$ ) is
explanation:
$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \mathrm{A}=\left[\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{ij}}\right]_{3 \times 3}, \text { where } \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{ij}}=2 \mathrm{i}+\mathrm{j} \\ \therefore& \mathrm{a}_{11}=2(1)+1=3 \\ & \mathrm{a}_{12}=2(1)+2=4 \\ & \mathrm{a}_{13}=2(1)+3=5 \\ & \mathrm{a}_{21}=2(2)+1=5 \\ & \mathrm{a}_{22}=2(2)+2=6 \\ & \mathrm{a}_{23}=2(2)+3=7 \\ & \mathrm{a}_{31}=2(3)+1=7 \\ & \mathrm{a}_{32}=2(3)+2=8 \\ & \mathrm{a}_{33}=2(3)+3=9 \\ \therefore & \mathrm{~A}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 3 & 4 & 5 \\ 5 & 6 & 7 \\ 7 & 8 & 9 \end{array}\right] \end{array} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Third element in the second row of adjoint of matrix A

$$ =(-1)^{3+2}\left|\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 5 \\ 5 & 7 \end{array}\right|=-1(-4)=4 $$

content: If $\left[\begin{array}{lll}1 & 3 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 & 4 \\ 1 & 3 & 4\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l}x \\ y \\ z\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l}12 \\ 15 \\ 13\end{array}\right]$, then the value of $x^2+y^2+z^2=$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{AX}=\mathrm{B} \\ & \Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 3 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 & 4 \\ 1 & 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 12 \\ 15 \\ 13 \end{array}\right] \end{aligned} $$

Applying $\mathrm{R}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_2-\mathrm{R}_1$ and $\mathrm{R}_3 \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_3-\mathrm{R}_1$,

$$ \left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 3 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 12 \\ 3 \\ 1 \end{array}\right] $$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & x+3 y+3 z=12 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ & y+z=3 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\\ & z=1 \end{array} $$

From (ii),

$$ y+1=3 \Rightarrow y=2 $$

From (i),

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & x+6+3=12 \Rightarrow x=3 \\ \therefore & x^2+y^2+z^2=9+4+1=14 \end{array} $$

content: If $A=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & \tan x \\ -\tan x & 1\end{array}\right]$, then $A^T A^{-1}=$
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{A} & =\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & \tan x \\ -\tan x & 1 \end{array}\right] \\ \mathrm{A}^{\top} & =\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -\tan x \\ \tan x & 1 \end{array}\right] \\ \mathrm{A}^{-1} & =\frac{\operatorname{adjA}}{|\mathrm{A}|}=\frac{1}{1+\tan ^2 x}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -\tan x \\ \tan x & 1 \end{array}\right] \\ \mathrm{A}^{\mathrm{T}} \mathrm{~A}^{-1} & =\frac{1}{1+\tan ^2 x}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -\tan x \\ \tan x & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -\tan x \\ \tan x & 1 \end{array}\right] \\ & =\left[\begin{array}{ll} \frac{1-\tan ^2 x}{1+\tan ^2 x} & \frac{-2 \tan x}{1+\tan ^2 x} \\ \frac{2 \tan ^2 x}{1+\tan ^2 x} & \frac{1-\tan ^2 x}{1+\tan ^2 x} \end{array}\right] \\ & =\left[\begin{array}{ll} \cos 2 x & -\sin 2 x \\ \sin 2 x & \cos 2 x \end{array}\right] \end{aligned} $$

content: If $A=\left[\begin{array}{rr}1 & 2 \\ -1 & 4\end{array}\right]$ and $A^{-1}=\alpha I+\beta A \alpha, \beta \in R$ where I is the identity matrix of order 2 , then $4(\alpha+\beta)=$
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{ll} & \text { If } A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} a & b \\ c & d \end{array}\right] \text { and } a d-b c \neq 0, \text { then } \\ & A^{-1}=\frac{1}{a d-b c}\left[\begin{array}{cc} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{array}\right] \\ & |A|=\left|\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ -1 & 4 \end{array}\right|=6 \neq 0 \\ \therefore & A^{-1}=\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 4 & -2 \\ 1 & 1 \end{array}\right] \\ & A^{-1}=\alpha I+\beta A \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 4 & -2 \\ 1 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\alpha\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]+\beta\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ -1 & 4 \end{array}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc} \frac{4}{6} & \frac{-2}{6} \\ \frac{1}{6} & \frac{1}{6} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \alpha+\beta & 2 \beta \\ -\beta & \alpha+4 \beta \end{array}\right] \end{array}\\ &\therefore \quad \text { by the equality of matrices, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \alpha+\beta=\frac{4}{6}=\frac{2}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow 4(\alpha+\beta)=4\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)=\frac{8}{3} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content:
If $A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}\cos \theta & \sin \theta & 0 \\ -\sin \theta & \cos \theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1\end{array}\right]$,
where $A_{21}, A_{22}, A_{23}$ are cofactors of $a_{21}, a_{22}, a_{23}$ respectively, then the value of $\mathrm{a}_{21} \mathrm{~A}_{21}+\mathrm{a}_{22} \mathrm{~A}_{22}+\mathrm{a}_{23} \mathrm{~A}_{23}=$
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \cos \theta & \sin \theta & 0 \\ -\sin \theta & \cos \theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] \\ & A_{21}=(-1)^{2+1} M_{21}=(-1)^3\left|\begin{array}{cc} \sin \theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right|=-\sin \theta \\ & A_{22}=(-1)^{2+2} M_{22}=(-1)^4\left|\begin{array}{cc} \cos \theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right|=\cos \theta \\ & A_{23}=(-1)^{2+3} M_{23}=(-1)^{2+3}\left|\begin{array}{cc} \cos \theta & \sin \theta \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right|=0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & a_{21} A_{21}+a_{22} A_{22}+a_{23} A_{23} \\ & =-\sin \theta(-\sin \theta)+\cos \theta \cos \theta+0 \\ & =\sin ^2 \theta+\cos ^2 \theta \\ & =1 \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $A=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & 2 \\ -1 & 4\end{array}\right]$ and $A^{-1}=\alpha \mathrm{I}+\beta \mathrm{A}, \alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{R}$, I is the identity matrix of order 2 , then $4(\alpha-\beta)$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &|A|=\left|\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ -1 & 4 \end{array}\right|=4+2=6 \neq 0\\ &\text { If } A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} a & b \\ c & d \end{array}\right] \text { and } \mathrm{ad}-\mathrm{bc} \neq 0 \text {, then }\\ &\begin{array}{l} & A^{-1} =\frac{1}{a d-b c}\left[\begin{array}{cc} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{array}\right] \\ \therefore \quad A^{-1} & =\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 4 & -2 \\ 1 & 1 \end{array}\right] \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & A^{-1}=\alpha I+\beta A \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 4 & -2 \\ 1 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \alpha & 0 \\ 0 & \alpha \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc} \beta & 2 \beta \\ -\beta & 4 \beta \end{array}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc} \frac{2}{3} & -\frac{1}{3} \\ \frac{1}{6} & \frac{1}{6} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \alpha+\beta & 2 \beta \\ -\beta & \alpha+4 \beta \end{array}\right] \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\therefore \quad \text { By the equality of matrices, }\\ &\begin{array}{ll} & \frac{1}{6}=-\beta \text { and } \alpha+\beta=\frac{2}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \beta=\frac{-1}{6} \text { and } \alpha-\frac{1}{6}=\frac{2}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \beta=\frac{-1}{6} \text { and } \alpha=\frac{5}{6} \\ \therefore \quad & 4(\alpha-\beta)=4 \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\bar{a}=a_1 \hat{i}+a_2 \hat{j}+a_3 \hat{k}, \quad \bar{b}=b_1 \hat{i}+b_2 \hat{j}+b_3 \hat{k}$, $\bar{c}=c_1 \hat{i}+c_2 \hat{j}+c_3 \hat{k}$ and $\left[\begin{array}{lll}3 \bar{a}+\bar{b} & 3 \bar{b}+\bar{c} & 3 \bar{c}+\bar{a}\end{array}\right]=\lambda\left|\begin{array}{lll}\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ \overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ \overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{k}}\end{array}\right|,$ then the value of $\lambda$ is
explanation:
$\lambda\left|\begin{array}{lll}\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ \overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ \overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{i}} & \overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \overline{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{k}}\end{array}\right|=\lambda\left|\begin{array}{lll}\mathrm{a}_1 & \mathrm{a}_2 & \mathrm{a}_3 \\ \mathrm{~b}_1 & \mathrm{~b}_2 & \mathrm{~b}_3 \\ \mathrm{c}_1 & \mathrm{c}_2 & \mathrm{c}_3\end{array}\right|=\lambda\left[\begin{array}{lll}\mathrm{a} & \mathrm{b} & \mathrm{c}\end{array}\right]$

$$\begin{aligned} & {[3 \overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}} \quad 3 \overline{\mathrm{~b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}} \quad 3 \overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{a}}]} \\ & =(3 \overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}) \cdot((3 \overline{\mathrm{~b}}+\overline{\mathrm{c}}) \times(3 \overline{\mathrm{c}}+\overline{\mathrm{a}})) \\ & =(3 \overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}) \cdot(9(\overline{\mathrm{~b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})+3(\overline{\mathrm{~b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}})+3(\overline{\mathrm{c}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})+(\overline{\mathrm{c}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}})) \\ & =(3 \overline{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}) \cdot(9(\overline{\mathrm{~b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})+3(\overline{\mathrm{~b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}})+0+(\overline{\mathrm{c}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}})) \\ & =27 \overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{~b}} \times \overline{\mathrm{c}})+0+0+0+0+\overline{\mathrm{b}} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{c}} \times \overline{\mathrm{a}}) \\ & =27\left[\begin{array}{lll} \overline{\mathrm{a}} & \overline{\mathrm{~b}} & \overline{\mathrm{c}}] \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{lll} \overline{\mathrm{b}} & \overline{\mathrm{c}} & \overline{\mathrm{a}} \end{array}\right] \\ & =27\left[\begin{array}{lll} \overline{\mathrm{a}} & \overline{\mathrm{~b}} & \overline{\mathrm{c}} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{lll} \overline{\mathrm{a}} & \overline{\mathrm{~b}} & \overline{\mathrm{c}} \end{array}\right] \\ & =28\left[\begin{array}{lll} \overline{\mathrm{a}} & \overline{\mathrm{~b}} & \overline{\mathrm{c}} \end{array}\right] \\ \therefore\quad& \lambda=28 \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $A=\left[\begin{array}{ll}x & 1 \\ 1 & 0\end{array}\right], x \in \mathbb{R}^{+}$and $A^4=\left[a_{i j}\right]_2$. If $a_{11}=109$, then $\left(A^4\right)^{-1}=$
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} x & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right] \\ & \mathrm{A}^2=\left[\begin{array}{ll} x & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} x & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} x^2+1 & x \\ x & 1 \end{array}\right] \\ & \mathrm{A}^4=\mathrm{A}^2 \cdot \mathrm{~A}^2 \\ &=\left[\begin{array}{cc} x^2+1 & x \\ x & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} x^2+1 & x \\ x & 1 \end{array}\right] \\ &=\left[\begin{array}{ll} \left(x^2+1\right)^2+x^2 & x\left(x^2+1+1\right) \\ x\left(x^2+1+1\right) & \left(x^2+1\right) \end{array}\right] \\ &=\left[\begin{array}{ll} \left(x^2+1\right)^2+x^2 & x\left(x^2+2\right) \\ x\left(x^2+2\right) & x^2+1 \end{array}\right] \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{A}^4=\left[\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{ij}}\right] \text { and } \mathrm{a}_{11}=109 \quad\text{.... [Given]}\\ & \mathrm{a}_{11}=109 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(x^2+1\right)^2+x^2=109 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { i.e. } \left(x^2+1\right)^2+x^2 & =100+9 \\ & =(10)^2+3^2=\left(3^2+1\right)^2+3^2 \end{aligned}$$

Comparing we get,

$$x^2=9 \Rightarrow x=3$$

$\mathrm{a}_{12}=x\left(x^2+2\right)=3(9+2)=33$

$\mathrm{a}_{21}=x\left(x^2+2\right)=3(9+2)=33$

$\mathrm{a}_{22}=x^2+1=9+1=10$

$\therefore \quad A^4=\left[\begin{array}{rr}109 & 33 \\ 33 & 10\end{array}\right]$

$\therefore \quad\left|A^4\right|=1 \neq 0$

If $A=\left[\begin{array}{ll}a & b \\ c & d\end{array}\right]$ and $\mathrm{ad}-\mathrm{bc} \neq 0$, then

$$\begin{aligned} & A^{-1}=\frac{1}{a d-b c}\left[\begin{array}{cc} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{array}\right] \\ \therefore & \left(A^4\right)^{-1}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 10 & -33 \\ -33 & 109 \end{array}\right] \end{aligned}$$

content: If $A=\left[\begin{array}{cc}5 a & -b \\ 3 & 2\end{array}\right]$ and $A \cdot \operatorname{adj} A=A A^T$, then $5 a+b$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{ll} & \mathrm{A}_{11}=(-1)^{1+1}(2)=2, \mathrm{~A}_{12}=(-1)^{1+2}(3)=-3, \\ & \mathrm{~A}_{21}=(-1)^{2+1}(-\mathrm{b})=\mathrm{b}, \mathrm{~A}_{22}=(-1)^{2+2}(5 \mathrm{a})=5 \mathrm{a} \\ \therefore & \text { adj } \mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & -3 \\ \mathrm{~b} & 5 \mathrm{a} \end{array}\right]^{\mathrm{T}}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & \mathrm{~b} \\ -3 & 5 \mathrm{a} \end{array}\right] \\ & \text { Given, } \mathrm{A} \text { adj } \mathrm{A}=\mathrm{AA}^{\mathrm{T}} \\ & \Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 \mathrm{a} & -\mathrm{b} \\ 3 & 2 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & \mathrm{~b} \\ -3 & 5 \mathrm{a} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 \mathrm{a} & -\mathrm{b} \\ 3 & 2 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 \mathrm{a} & 3 \\ -\mathrm{b} & 2 \end{array}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc} 10 \mathrm{a}+3 \mathrm{~b} & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \mathrm{~b}+10 \mathrm{a} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 25 \mathrm{a}^2+\mathrm{b}^2 & 15 \mathrm{a}-2 \mathrm{~b} \\ 15 \mathrm{a}-2 \mathrm{~b} & 13 \end{array}\right] \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\therefore \quad \text { by the equality of matrices, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & 15 a-2 b=0 \text { and } 3 b+10 a=13 \\ & \Rightarrow a=\frac{2}{5} \text { and } b=3 \\ \therefore \quad & 5 a+b=5\left(\frac{2}{5}\right)+3=2+3=5 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: Let A and B be $3 \times 3$ real matrices such that A is symmetric matrix and B is skew-symmetric matrix. Then the system of linear equations $\left(A^2 B^2-B^2 A^2\right) X=O$. where $X$ is $3 \times 1$ column matrix of unknown variables and $O$ is a $3 \times 1$ null matrix, has
explanation:
Let $\mathrm{P}=\mathrm{A}^2 \mathrm{~B}^2-\mathrm{B}^2 \mathrm{~A}^2$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{P}^T & =\left(\mathrm{A}^2 \mathrm{~B}^2-\mathrm{B}^2 \mathrm{~A}^2\right)^{\mathrm{T}} \\ & =\left(\mathrm{A}^2 \mathrm{~B}^2\right)^{\mathrm{T}}-\left(\mathrm{B}^2 \mathrm{~A}^2\right)^{\mathrm{T}} \\ & =\left(\mathrm{B}^2\right)^{\mathrm{T}}\left(\mathrm{~A}^2\right)^{\mathrm{T}}-\left(\mathrm{A}^2\right)^{\mathrm{T}}\left(\mathrm{~B}^2\right)^{\mathrm{T}} \\ & =\mathrm{B}^2 \mathrm{~A}^2-\mathrm{A}^2 \mathrm{~B}^2 \quad \cdots\left[\because \mathrm{~A}^{\mathrm{T}}=\mathrm{A} \text { and } \mathrm{B}^{\mathrm{T}}=-\mathrm{B}\right] \\ & =-\left(\mathrm{A}^2 \mathrm{~B}^2-\mathrm{B}^2 \mathrm{~A}^2\right) \\ & =-\mathrm{P} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad P$ is a skew - symmetric matrix.

$\therefore \quad \operatorname{det}(\mathrm{P})=0$

$\therefore \quad$ The given system of equations has infinitely many solutions.

content: If $A\left[\begin{array}{ll}2 & 1 \\ 7 & 4\end{array}\right]$ then $\left(A^2-5 A\right)^{-1}$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 1 \\ 7 & 4 \end{array}\right] \\ \therefore\quad& A^2=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 1 \\ 7 & 4 \end{array}\right] \times\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 1 \\ 7 & 4 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 11 & 6 \\ 42 & 23 \end{array}\right] \\ \therefore\quad& A^2-5 A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 1 \\ 7 & 3 \end{array}\right] \\ & \left|A^2-5 A\right|=3-7=-4 \\ \therefore\quad & \left(A^2-5 A\right)^{-1} =\left(-\frac{1}{4}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & -1 \\ -7 & 1 \end{array}\right]\right. \\ & \quad=\frac{1}{4}\left[\begin{array}{cc} -3 & 1 \\ 7 & -1 \end{array}\right] \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & -1 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & -3 \\ 1 & 1 & 1\end{array}\right]$ and $B=\left[\begin{array}{l}4 \\ 0 \\ 2\end{array}\right]$ such that $\mathrm{AX}=\mathrm{B}$, then $\mathrm{X}=$
explanation:

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Applying } R_2 \rightarrow R_2-2 R_1 \text { and } R_3 \rightarrow R_3-R_1 \text {, }\\ &\begin{array}{ll} & {\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & 1 \\ 0 & 3 & -5 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 4 \\ -8 \\ -2 \end{array}\right]} \\ \therefore \quad & x_1-x_2+x_3=4 \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ & 3 x_2-5 x_3=-8 \quad\text{.... (ii)}\\ & 2 x_2=-2 \Rightarrow x_2=-1 \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\mathrm{w}=\frac{-1-\mathrm{i} \sqrt{3}}{2}$ where $\mathrm{i}=\sqrt{-1}$, then the value of $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & w & w^2 \\ w & w^2 & 1 \\ w^2 & 1 & w\end{array}\right|$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \omega=\frac{-1-\mathrm{i} \sqrt{3}}{2}, \omega^2=\frac{1-3+2 \mathrm{i} \sqrt{3}}{4}=\frac{-1+\mathrm{i} \sqrt{3}}{2}, \\ & \omega^3=1, \omega^6=1 \\ & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & \omega & \omega^2 \\ \omega & \omega^2 & 1 \\ \omega^2 & 1 & \omega \end{array}\right| \\ & =1\left(\omega^3-1\right)-\omega\left(\omega^2-\omega^2\right)+\omega^2\left(\omega-\omega^4\right) \\ & =1\left(\omega^3-1\right)+0+\omega^2\left(\omega-\omega^4\right) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\omega^3-1+\omega^3-\omega^6 \\ & =2 \omega^3-\omega^6-1 \\ & =2(1)-1-1 \\ & =0 \end{aligned}$$

content: Inverse of the matrix $\left[\begin{array}{cc}0.8 & -0.6 \\ 0.6 & 0.8\end{array}\right]$ is

content: If $A+B=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & \tan \frac{\theta}{2} \\ -\tan \frac{\theta}{2} & 1\end{array}\right]$ where $A$ is symmetric and $B$ is skew-symmetric matrix, then the matrix $\left(A^{-1} B+A B^{-1}\right)$ at $\theta=\frac{\pi}{6}$ is given by
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Given, } A+B=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & \tan \frac{\theta}{2} \\ -\tan \frac{\theta}{2} & 1 \end{array}\right] \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ &A^T+B^T=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -\tan \frac{\theta}{2}\\ \tan \frac{\theta}{2} & 1 \end{array}\right] \end{aligned}$$

Since $A$ is symmetric and $B$ is skew symmetric

$$\begin{aligned} & A^T=A, B^T=-B \\ \therefore & A-B=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -\tan \frac{\theta}{2} \\ \tan \frac{\theta}{2} & 1 \end{array}\right]\quad\text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

Adding (i) and (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} 2 A & =\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & \tan \frac{\theta}{2} \\ -\tan \frac{\theta}{2} & 1 \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -\tan \frac{\theta}{2} \\ \tan \frac{\theta}{2} & 1 \end{array}\right] \\ 2 A & =\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{array}\right] \\ \therefore \quad A & =\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Now, subtracting (ii) from (i), we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & 2 B=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & \tan \frac{\theta}{2} \\ -\tan \frac{\theta}{2} & 1 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -\tan \frac{\theta}{2} \\ \tan \frac{\theta}{2} & 1 \end{array}\right] \\ & 2 B=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 0 & 2 \tan \frac{\theta}{2} \\ -2 \tan \frac{\theta}{2} & 0 \end{array}\right] \\ \therefore & B=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 0 & \tan \frac{\theta}{2} \\ -\tan \frac{\theta}{2} & 0 \end{array}\right] \\ \therefore & B^{-1}=\frac{1}{\tan ^2 \frac{\theta}{2}}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 0 & -\tan \frac{\theta}{2} \\ \tan \frac{\theta}{2} & 0 \end{array}\right] \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad B^{-1}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 0 & \frac{-1}{\tan \frac{\theta}{2}} \\ \frac{1}{\tan \frac{\theta}{2}} & 0 \end{array}\right] \\ & \therefore \quad \text { Now, }\left(\mathrm{A}^{-1} \mathrm{~B}+\mathrm{AB}^{-1}\right) \end{aligned}$$

$=\left[\begin{array}{ll}1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}0 & \tan \frac{\theta}{2} \\ -\tan \frac{\theta}{2} & 0\end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ll}1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}0 & \frac{-1}{\tan \frac{\theta}{2}} \\ \frac{1}{\tan \frac{\theta}{2}} & 0\end{array}\right]$

$=\left[\begin{array}{cc}0 & \tan \frac{\theta}{2} \\ -\tan \frac{\theta}{2} & 0\end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc}0 & \frac{-1}{\tan \frac{\theta}{2}} \\ \frac{1}{\tan \frac{\theta}{2}} & 0\end{array}\right]$

$=\left[\begin{array}{cc}0 & \frac{\tan ^2 \frac{\theta}{2}-1}{\tan \frac{\theta}{2}} \\ \frac{1-\tan ^2 \frac{\theta}{2}}{\tan \frac{\theta}{2}} & 0\end{array}\right]$

$=2\left[\begin{array}{cc}0 & (-1) \frac{1-\tan ^2 \frac{\theta}{2}}{2 \tan \frac{\theta}{2}} \\ \frac{1-\tan ^2 \frac{\theta}{2}}{2 \tan \frac{\theta}{2}} & 0\end{array}\right]$

$$\begin{aligned} & =2\left[\begin{array}{cc} 0 & \frac{-1}{\tan \theta} \\ \frac{1}{\tan \theta} & 0 \end{array}\right] \\ \therefore & \left(\mathrm{A}^{-1} \mathrm{~B}+\mathrm{AB}^{-1}\right) \text { at } \theta=\frac{\pi}{6} \\ & =2\left[\begin{array}{cc} 0 & \frac{-1}{\tan \frac{\pi}{6}} \\ \frac{1}{\tan \frac{\pi}{6}} & 0 \end{array}\right] \\ & =2\left[\begin{array}{cc} 0 & -\sqrt{3} \\ \sqrt{3} & 0 \end{array}\right] \\ & =\left[\begin{array}{cc} 0 & -2 \sqrt{3} \\ 2 \sqrt{3} & 0 \end{array}\right] \end{aligned}$$

content: For the matrix $A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}2 & 0 & -1 \\ 3 & 1 & 2 \\ -1 & 1 & 2\end{array}\right]$, the matrix of cofactors is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{A}_{11}=(-1)^{1+1}\left|\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 \end{array}\right|=1(0)=0 \\ & \mathrm{~A}_{12}=(-1)^{1+2}\left|\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 2 \\ -1 & 2 \end{array}\right|=(-1)(8)=-8 \\ & \mathrm{~A}_{13}=(-1)^{1+3}\left|\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{array}\right|=(1)(4)=4 \\ & \mathrm{~A}_{21}=(-1)^{2+1}\left|\begin{array}{cc} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{array}\right|=(-1)(1)=-1 \\ & \mathrm{~A}_{22}=(-1)^{2+2}\left|\begin{array}{cc} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{array}\right|=(1)(3)=3 \\ & \mathrm{~A}_{23}=(-1)^{2+3}\left|\begin{array}{cc} 2 & 0 \\ -1 & 1 \end{array}\right|=(-1)(2)=-2 \\ & \mathrm{~A}_{31}=(-1)^{3+1}\left|\begin{array}{cc} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{array}\right|=(1)(1)=1 \\ & \mathrm{~A}_{32}=(-1)^{3+2}\left|\begin{array}{cc} 2 & -1 \\ 3 & 2 \end{array}\right|=(-1)(7)=-7 \\ & \mathrm{~A}_{33}=(-1)^{3+3}\left|\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 0 \\ 3 & 1 \end{array}\right|=(1)(2)=2 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\therefore \quad \text { The matrix of the cofactors is }\\ &\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & -8 & 4 \\ -1 & 3 & -2 \\ 1 & -7 & 2 \end{array}\right] \end{aligned}$$

content: If $A=\left[\begin{array}{lll}1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & a & 3 \\ 3 & 2 & 2\end{array}\right]$ and $B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}-2 & 0 & b \\ 7 & -1 & -2 \\ c & 1 & 1\end{array}\right]$ and if matrix $B$ is the inverse of matrix $A$, then value of $4 a+2 b-c$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & B=A^{-1} \\ & {\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -2 & 0 & b \\ 7 & -1 & -2 \\ c & 1 & 1 \end{array}\right]=A^{-1}} \\ & {\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -2 & 0 & b \\ 7 & -1 & -2 \\ c & 1 & 1 \end{array}\right] A=A^{-1} A} \\ & {\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -2 & 0 & b \\ 7 & -1 & -2 \\ c & 1 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & a & 3 \\ 3 & 2 & 2 \end{array}\right]=I} \\ & {\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -2+3 b & -2+2 b & -2+2 b \\ 7-1-6 & 7-a-4 & 7-3-4 \\ c+1+3 & c+a+2 & c+3+2 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]} \end{aligned}$$

On equality of matrix,

$$\begin{aligned} & -2+3 b=1,7-a-4=1, c+3+2=1 \\ \Rightarrow b=1, a & =2, c=-4 \\ \therefore \quad 4 a+2 b-c & =4(2)+2(1)+4 \\ & =8+2+4=14 \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $\mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & 2 \\ -5 & 1\end{array}\right]$ and $\mathrm{A}^{-1}=x \mathrm{~A}+y \mathrm{I}_2$, (where $\mathrm{I}_2$ is unit matrix of order 2), then
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{rlr} & |A|=\left|\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ -5 & 1 \end{array}\right|=11 \neq 0 \\ \therefore & A^{-1}=\frac{1}{11}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -2 \\ 5 & 1 \end{array}\right] \end{array}\\ &\ldots \text { [ If } A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} a & b \\ c & d \end{array}\right] \text { and } a d-b c \neq 0 \text {, then }\\ &\left.\mathrm{A}^{-1}=\frac{1}{(\mathrm{ad}-\mathrm{bc})}\left[\begin{array}{cc} \mathrm{d} & -\mathrm{b} \\ -\mathrm{c} & \mathrm{a} \end{array}\right] .\right] \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \mathrm{A}^{-1}=x \mathrm{~A}+y \mathrm{I} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{1}{11}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -2 \\ 5 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} x & 2 x \\ -5 x & x \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ll} y & 0 \\ 0 & y \end{array}\right] \\ \therefore \quad & {\left[\begin{array}{cc} \frac{1}{11} & \frac{-2}{11} \\ \frac{5}{11} & \frac{1}{11} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} x+y & 2 x \\ -5 x & x+y \end{array}\right]} \end{array}$$

$\therefore \quad$ By the equality of matrices,

$2 x=\frac{-2}{11}$ and $x+y=\frac{1}{11}$

$\therefore\quad x=\frac{-1}{11}$ and $\frac{-1}{11}+y=\frac{1}{11}$

$\therefore\quad x=\frac{-1}{11}$ and $y=\frac{2}{11}$

content: Suppose A is any $3 \times 3$ non-singular matrix and $(\mathrm{A}-3 \mathrm{I})(\mathrm{A}-5 \mathrm{I})=0$ where $\mathrm{I}=\mathrm{I}_3$ and $\mathrm{O}=\mathrm{O}_3$. Here $\mathrm{O}_3$ represent zero matrix of order 3 and $\mathrm{I}_3$ is an identity matrix of order 3 . If $\alpha A+\beta A^{-1}=4 I$, then $\alpha+\beta$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{array}{ll} & \text { Given that }(A-3 I)(A-5 I)=0 \\ \therefore & A^2-3 A-5 A+15 I=0 \\ \therefore & A^2-8 A+15 I=0 \\ \therefore & A^2+15 I=8 A \end{array}$$

Multiplying entire equation by $\frac{\mathrm{A}^{-1}}{2}$, we get

$$\frac{1}{2} A+\frac{15}{2} A^{-1}=4 I$$

Comparing with $\alpha \mathrm{A}+\beta \mathrm{A}^{-1}=4 \mathrm{I}$, we get

$\alpha=\frac{1}{2}$ and $\beta=\frac{15}{2}$

$$\therefore \quad \alpha+\beta=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{15}{2}=8$$

content: For the system $x-y+z=4,2 x+y-3 z=0$, $x+y+z=2$, the values of $x, y, z$ respectively are given by
explanation:
Given equation in matrix form

$$\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & -3 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 4 \\ 0 \\ 2 \end{array}\right]$$

It is of the form $A X=B$

Now, $|A|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & -1 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & -3 \\ 1 & 1 & 1\end{array}\right|=10 \neq 0$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & A^{-1} \text { exist } \\ & \operatorname{adj} A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 2 & 2 \\ -5 & 0 & 5 \\ 1 & -2 & 3 \end{array}\right] \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} A^{-1} & =\frac{1}{|A|} \operatorname{adj} A \\ & =\frac{1}{10}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 2 & 2 \\ -5 & 0 & 5 \\ 1 & -2 & 3 \end{array}\right] \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \text { Now, } A X=B \\ \therefore \quad & A^{-1}(A X)=A^{-1} B \\ \therefore \quad & X=A^{-1} B \end{array}$$

explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ -5 & 1 \end{array}\right] \\ & \therefore|\mathrm{A}|=11 \\ & A^{-1}=\frac{1}{|A|}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -2 \\ 5 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{11}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -2 \\ 5 & 1 \end{array}\right] \\ & \mathrm{A}^{-1}=x \mathrm{~A}+y \mathrm{I} \text {, we get } \\ & {\left[\begin{array}{cc} \frac{1}{11} & \frac{-2}{11} \\ \frac{5}{11} & \frac{1}{11} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} x & 2 x \\ -5 x & x \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ll} y & 0 \\ 0 & y \end{array}\right]} \\ & \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & {\left[\begin{array}{cc} \frac{1}{11} & \frac{-2}{11} \\ \frac{5}{11} & \frac{1}{11} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} x+y & 2 x \\ -5 x & x+y \end{array}\right]} \\ \Rightarrow & x=\frac{-1}{11} \text { and } y=\frac{2}{11} \\ \therefore & 2 x+3 y=2\left(\frac{-1}{11}\right)+3\left(\frac{2}{11}\right)=\frac{4}{11} \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} i & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right] \\ & \therefore \quad A^2=\left[\begin{array}{ll} i & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right] \times\left[\begin{array}{ll} i & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & i \\ i & 1 \end{array}\right] \\ & \therefore \quad A^3=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & i \\ i & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} i & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} i & 0 \\ 0 & i \end{array}\right] \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{A}^6=\mathrm{A}^3 \times \mathrm{A}^3 \\ & =\left[\begin{array}{ll} \mathrm{i} & 0 \\ 0 & \mathrm{i} \end{array}\right] \times\left[\begin{array}{ll} \mathrm{i} & 0 \\ 0 & \mathrm{i} \end{array}\right] \\ & =\left[\begin{array}{cc} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{array}\right]=(-1) I_2 \\ & \text { Now, } B=A^{2029}=A^{(6 \times 338+1)} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{B}=\left(\mathrm{A}^6\right)^{338} \times \mathrm{A} \\ & =\left((-1) I_2\right)^{338} \times A \\ & =\mathrm{I}_2 \times \mathrm{A} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{B}=\mathrm{A} \\ & \text { Now, }|A|=-1 \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{B}^{-1}=\mathrm{A}^{-1}=\frac{1}{|\mathrm{~A}|} \operatorname{adj} \mathrm{A} \\ & \therefore \quad B^{-1}=-\operatorname{adj} \mathrm{A} \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $$\omega \neq 1$$ be a cube root of unity and $$S$$ be the set of all non-singular matrices of the form $$\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & a & b \\ \omega & 1 & c \\ \omega^2 & \omega & 1\end{array}\right]$$ where each of $$a, b$$ and $$c$$ is either $$\omega$$ or $$\omega^2$$, then the number of distinct matrices in the set $$\mathrm{S}$$ is
explanation:
Let $$A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & a & b \\ \omega & 1 & c \\ \omega^2 & \omega & 1\end{array}\right]$$

For non-singular matrix $$|A| \neq 0$$

$$\Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & a & b \\ \omega & 1 & c \\ \omega^2 & \omega & 1 \end{array}\right| \neq 0$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 1(1-\omega c)-a\left(\omega-\omega^2 c\right)+b(0) \neq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow 1(1-\omega c)-a \omega(1-\omega c) \neq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow(1-\omega c)(1-a \omega) \neq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow c \neq \frac{1}{\omega} \text { and } a \neq \frac{1}{\omega} \\ & \Rightarrow c \neq \omega^2 \text { and } a \neq \omega^2 \quad \ldots\left[\because \omega^3=1\right] \end{aligned}$$

So possible value of $$a$$ and $$c$$ is $$\omega$$ only and $$b$$ can take values $$\omega$$ or $$\omega^2$$.

$$\therefore \quad$$ The possible number of distinct matrices = 2.

content: Given $$A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}x & 3 & 2 \\ 1 & y & 4 \\ 2 & 2 & z\end{array}\right]$$, if $$x y z=60$$ and $$8 x+4 y+3 z=20$$, then $$A$$.(adjA)
explanation:
To find the matrix product $$A \cdot (\text{adj}A)$$, we first need to understand the properties of a matrix and its adjugate (adjoint) matrix. The adjugate of a matrix $$A$$, denoted as $$\text{adj}A$$, is the transpose of the cofactor matrix of $$A$$. One important property relating a matrix and its adjugate involves the determinant of the matrix, given by the equation:

$$A \cdot (\text{adj}A) = (\text{det}A) \cdot I$$

where $$I$$ is the identity matrix of the same size as $$A$$, and $$\text{det}A$$ is the determinant of the matrix $$A$$. This relation tells us that the product of a matrix and its adjugate is a diagonal matrix, where each diagonal element is equal to the determinant of the original matrix.

Given the matrix $$A$$ and the equations $$xyz = 60$$ and $$8x + 4y + 3z = 20$$, we are asked to find $$A \cdot (\text{adj}A)$$. According to the property mentioned, this requires us to find the determinant of the given matrix $$A$$.

The determinant of $$A$$, $$\text{det}A$$, for a 3x3 matrix $$\left[\begin{array}{ccc}a & b & c \\ d & e & f \\ g & h & i\end{array}\right]$$ is given by:

$$\text{det}A = a(ei - fh) - b(di - fg) + c(dh - eg)$$

Substituting the values from matrix $$A$$, we get:

$$\text{det}A = x(yz - 8) - 3(z - 8) + 2(2 - 2y)$$

$$\text{det}A = xyz - 8x - 3z + 24 + 4 - 4y$$

$$\text{det}A = xyz - ( 8x + 4y + 3z ) + 24 + 4 $$

Since we know that $$xyz = 60$$ and have the equation $$8x + 4y + 3z = 20$$, we can substitute $$xyz = 60$$ and $$8x + 4y + 3z = 20$$ in ours determinant formula:

$$\text{det}A = 60 - 20 + 28 = 68$$

But to proceed, we notice that directly applying the properties will be more efficient. By knowing that $$\text{det}A = 68$$ (since it's given by the product $$xyz$$), all we need to use is the derived property of a matrix and its adjugate:

$$A \cdot (\text{adj}A) = (\text{det}A) \cdot I$$

Given that $$\text{det}A = 68$$, the right hand side of this equation becomes:

$$A \cdot (\text{adj}A) = 68 \cdot I$$

Where $$I$$ is the identity matrix of the same dimensions as $$A$$, which is a 3x3 matrix. Therefore,

$$A \cdot (\text{adj}A) = \left[\begin{array}{ccc}68 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 68 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 68\end{array}\right]$$

This matches with Option D. The correct answer is therefore Option D:

$$\left[\begin{array}{ccc}68 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 68 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 68\end{array}\right]$$

Since $$A^{-1}$$ does not exist, we write $$|A|=0$$

$$\therefore\left(-\lambda^2\right)-\left(i^2\right)=0 \quad \Rightarrow \lambda^2=1 \Rightarrow \lambda= \pm 1 \text {. }$$

$$|\mathrm{A}|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ -1 & 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 & 4 \end{array}\right|=1(0)-2(-6)+3(-3)=3$$

We know that $$\mathrm{A}(\operatorname{adj} \mathrm{A})=|\mathrm{A}| \mathrm{I}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \mathrm{A}(\operatorname{adj} \mathrm{A})=3 \mathrm{I} \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=3 \\ & (\mathrm{k}+1)^4=(3+1)^4=256 \end{aligned}$$

content: IF $$A X=B$$, where $$A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & -1 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & -3 \\ 1 & 1 & 1\end{array}\right], X=\left[\begin{array}{l}x \\ y \\ z\end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{l}4 \\ 0 \\ 2\end{array}\right]$$, then $$2 x+y-z=$$
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & {\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & -3 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 4 \\ 0 \\ 2 \end{array}\right]} \\ & R_3 \rightarrow R_3-R_1 \text { and } R_2 \rightarrow R_2-2 R_1 \\ & {\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & 1 \\ 0 & 3 & -5 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 4 \\ -8 \\ -2 \end{array}\right]} \end{aligned}$$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore x-y+z & =4 \quad \text{.... (1)}\\ 3 y-5 z & =-8 \quad \text{.... (2)}\\ 2 y & =-2 \quad \text{.... (3)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore y=-1 \quad \text {.....[from (3)]}\\ & \therefore-3-5 z=-8 \Rightarrow z=1 \quad \text {.....[from (2)]}\\ & \therefore x-(-1)+1=4 \Rightarrow x=2 \quad \text {.....[from (1)]}\\ & \therefore 2 x+y-z=2(2)-1-1=2 \end{aligned}$$

content: $$\text { If } A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & -2 \\ 2 & -3 \end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right] \text {, then }\left(B^{-1} A^{-1}\right)^{-1}=\text { ? }$$

explanation:
$$|A|=\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 1 & 1 & a \\ 2 & 4 & 7 \end{array}\right|=(7-4 a)-2(7-2 a)+3(2)=-1$$

Matrix B is inverse of matrix A. 'b' is element $$(1 \times 3)$$ in matrix B Here element $$(3 \times 1)$$ in matrix $$\mathrm{A}$$ is 2.

Cofactor of $$2=(-1)^{3+1}\left|\begin{array}{ll}2 & 3 \\ 1 & \mathrm{a}\end{array}\right|=2 \mathrm{a}-3$$

$$\text { Now } \frac{2 a-3}{-1}=b \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2 a+b=3 \quad \text{.... (1)}$$

For element $$(1 \times 2)$$ in matrix A i.e. 2.

Now cofactor of 2 is $$(-1)^{1+2}\left|\begin{array}{ll}1 & a \\ 2 & 7\end{array}\right|=-(7-2 a)$$ and $$\frac{-(7-2 a)}{-1}=7-2 a$$

Here element $$(2 \times 1)$$ in matrix B is $$-$$3

$$\therefore 7-2a=-3$$ .... (2)

Solving eq. (1) and (2), we get a = 5, b = $$-$$7

explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & {\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & 1 \\ 2 & -1 & 0 \\ 3 & 3 & -4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{array}\right]} \\ & R_3 \rightarrow R_3-3 R_1 \text { and } R_2 \rightarrow R_2-2 R_1 \\ & {\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & -2 \\ 0 & 6 & -7 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ -1 \\ -1 \end{array}\right]} \\ & \mathrm{R}_3 \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_3-6 \mathrm{R}_2 \\ & {\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 5 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ -1 \\ 5 \end{array}\right]} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{x}_1-\mathrm{x}_2+\mathrm{x}_3=1 \\ & \mathrm{x}_2-2 \mathrm{x}_3=-1 \\ & 5 x_3=5 \\ & \therefore \mathrm{x}_3=1, \mathrm{x}_2=1, \mathrm{x}_1=1 \\ \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 a & -b \\ 3 & 2 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & b \\ -3 & 5 a \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 a & -b \\ 3 & 2 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 a & 3 \\ -b & 2 \end{array}\right] \\ & {\left[\begin{array}{cc} 10 a+3 b & 0 \\ 0 & 10 a+3 b \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 25 a^2+b^2 & 15 a-2 b \\ 15 a-2 b & 9+4 \end{array}\right]} \\ & \therefore 10 a+3 b=13 \quad \text { and } 15 a-2 b=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$A=\left[\begin{array}{lll}1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 & 3 \\ 1 & 2 & 1\end{array}\right]$$, then the value of determinant of $$A^{-1}$$ is
If $$A = \left[ {\matrix{ k & 2 \cr { - 2} & { - k} \cr } } \right]$$, then A$$^{-1}$$ does not exists if k =
explanation:
To determine when the inverse of the matrix $ A $ does not exist, we need to find when the determinant of $ A $ is zero. For a 2x2 matrix of the form:

$$ A = \left[ {\matrix{ k & 2 \cr { - 2} & { - k} \cr } } \right] $$

the determinant can be calculated using the formula:

$$ \text{det}(A) = ad - bc $$

where $ a, b, c, $ and $ d $ are the elements of the matrix $ A $. Substituting the values from matrix $ A $:

$$ \text{det}(A) = k(-k) - (2)(-2) $$

Simplifying this expression, we get:

$$ \text{det}(A) = -k^2 + 4 $$

For the inverse of $ A $ to not exist, the determinant must be equal to zero:

$$ -k^2 + 4 = 0 $$

Solving for $ k $, we get:

$$ -k^2 + 4 = 0 $$

$$ k^2 = 4 $$

Taking the square root of both sides, we find:

$$ k = \pm 2 $$

Therefore, the inverse of matrix $ A $ does not exist when $ k = 2 $ or $ k = -2 $. Hence, the correct option is:

Option B $$\pm$$2

$$\therefore 3+y+\frac{4}{3}=6 \Rightarrow y=\frac{5}{3}$$ .....[From (1)]

content: If $$A=\left[\begin{array}{lll}0 & 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 3 & a & 1\end{array}\right]$$ and $$A^{-1}=\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & -1 & 1 \\ -8 & 6 & 2 c \\ 5 & -3 & 1\end{array}\right]$$, then values of a and c are respectively
explanation:
We know that $$\mathrm{AA}^{-1}=\mathrm{I}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore\left[\begin{array}{lll} 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 3 & a & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \frac{1}{2} & \frac{-1}{2} & \frac{1}{2} \\ -4 & 3 & c \\ \frac{5}{2} & \frac{-3}{2} & \frac{1}{2} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] \\ & \therefore\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & c+1 \\ 0 & 1 & 2+2 c \\ 4-4 a & 3 a-3 & 2+a c \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$F(\propto)=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}\cos \propto & -\sin \propto & 0 \\ \sin \propto & \cos \propto & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1\end{array}\right]$$, where $$\propto \in R$$, then $$[F(\propto)]^{-1}=$$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & F(\alpha)=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}\cos \alpha & -\sin \alpha & 0 \\ \sin \alpha & \cos \alpha & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1\end{array}\right] \\ & \therefore|F(\alpha)|=\cos \alpha(\cos \alpha)+\sin \alpha(\sin \alpha)=\cos ^2 \alpha+\sin ^2 \alpha=1 \\ & \therefore \operatorname{adj}[F(\alpha)]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}\cos \alpha & -\sin \alpha & 0 \\ \sin \alpha & \cos \alpha & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1\end{array}\right] \\ & \quad=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}\cos \alpha & \sin \alpha & 0 \\ -\sin \alpha & \cos \alpha & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}\cos (-\alpha) & -\sin (-\alpha) & 0 \\ \sin (-\alpha) & \cos (-\alpha) & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1\end{array}\right]=F(-\alpha) \\ & {[f(\alpha)]^{-1}=\frac{\operatorname{adj}[F(\alpha)]}{|F(\alpha)|}=F(-\alpha)}\end{aligned}$$

content: If $A=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1+2 i & i \\ -i & 1-2 i\end{array}\right]$, where $i=\sqrt{-1}$, then $A(\operatorname{adj} A)=\ldots$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { We have, } A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1+2 i & i \\ -i & 1-2 i \end{array}\right] \\ & \therefore|A|=\left|\begin{array}{cc} 1+2 i & i \\ -i & 1-2 i \end{array}\right|=(1+2 i)(1-2 i)+i^2 \\ & =1-(2 i)^2+(-1)=1+4-1=4 \\ & \therefore A(\operatorname{adj} A)=|A| I=4 I \end{aligned}$$

content: If $A=\left[\begin{array}{ll}x & 1 \\ 1 & 0\end{array}\right]$ and $A=A^{-1}$, then $x=\ldots \ldots$
explanation:
We have, $A=\left[\begin{array}{ll}x & 1 \\ 1 & 0\end{array}\right]$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad|A| & =(x \times 0-1)=-1 \\ \operatorname{adj} A & =\left[\begin{array}{cc} 0 & -1 \\ -1 & x \end{array}\right] \\ A^{-1} & =\frac{\operatorname{adj} A}{|A|}=(-1)\left[\begin{array}{cc} 0 & -1 \\ -1 & x \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & -x \end{array}\right] \end{aligned}$$

Since, It is given, $A=A^{-1}$

$$\Rightarrow \quad\left[\begin{array}{ll} x & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & -x \end{array}\right]$$

$\Rightarrow \quad x=0 \quad$ (By equality of matrices)

content: If $A$ is non-singular matrix such that $(A-2 l)(A-4 I)=0$ then $A+8 A^{-1}=$ ..........
explanation:
We have, $|A| \neq 0$

$\Rightarrow A^{-1}$ is exists.

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Since, }(A-2 I)(A-4 I)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad A^2-A(4 I)-2 I(A)+8 I \cdot I=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad A^2-4 A I-2 A I+8 I=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad A^2-6 A I+8 I=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad A^2-6 A+8 I=0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { On pre multiply both sides by } A^{-1} \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{array}{rrr} & A^{-1} A^2-6 A^{-1} A+8 A^{-1} I=0 \\ \Rightarrow & I A-6 I+8 A^{-1}=0 \\ \Rightarrow & A-6 I+8 A^{-1}=0 \\ \Rightarrow & A+8 A^{-1}=6 I \end{array} \end{aligned}$$
